The new device connects to a person's knee and captures energy when walking. The part about Larry Rome's device says it was a bag carried on the back that produces power from walking. The key difference noted in the text is: "The knee device does not produce as much electricity as the bag, But the bag requires the walker to carry a load of twenty to thirty kilograms." So the comparison here is that the knee device doesn't produce as much electricity as the bag, but the bag requires carrying a heavy load. The question is asking what the new device does compared to Rome's. So the new (knee) device's advantage is that it doesn't add more load, whereas the bag does require carrying a heavy load. Looking at the options: A) produces power without adding more loads to the walker. That matches the text's statement that the bag requires carrying 20-30 kg, while the knee device presumably doesn't add such a load. B) can produce more power in a much shorter time. No, the text says the knee device produces less electricity than the bag, so B is incorrect. C) needs to be equipped with a battery. The text doesn't mention anything about a battery for either device, so probably not. D) can help the walker walk faster.
